steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage hypothesizes the positive association between two variables is unique to North America, but Luo's China study and other non-North American studies all found the same positive association. The question asks which choice best explains why Finland is an exception to that broader pattern.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — environmental characteristics unique to Finland likely result in dissolved organic carbon and mercury levels in bodies of fresh water both being significantly higher there than elsewhere.: The hypothesis is about correlation, not absolute amounts. Absolute levels don't explain why the association disappears. ✗
  • Choice B — the bodies of water examined in Porvari and Verta's study may be more similar to the bodies of water examined in studies conducted in North America than to those examined in Luo and colleagues' study.: If true, Finland should show the same positive association as North America, making the absence of that association harder to explain, not easier. ✗
  • Choice C — there may be circumstances specific to the bodies of water examined in Porvari and Verta's study that disrupted a general positive association between dissolved organic carbon and mercury levels.: Treats Finland as a local exception driven by particular conditions, consistent with a global positive pattern that one region can deviate from. ✓
  • Choice D — bodies of fresh water outside North America are more likely to show a negative association between dissolved organic carbon and mercury levels than a positive association.: Contradicted directly by Luo's study and the other non-North American studies, which found positive associations. ✗
That confirms C is correct.
